plainMeaning: May the perfected, liberated souls (Siddhas) — more spotless than the moons, more radiant than the suns, and deep as the great ocean — grant me final liberation.
recitation: candesu nimmalayarā, āiccesu ahiyaṁ payāsayarā; sāgara-vara-gambhīrā siddhā siddhiṁ mama disantu.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. candra → Pkt. canda 'moon'; locative plural -esu, here functioning as the Prakrit 'comparative locative' ('than the moons').
wordByWord.notes: The locative of comparison: the Siddhas surpass even the moons.

wordByWord.etymology: nis + mala 'stain' → Pkt. nimmala 'stainless'; comparative suffix Skt. -tara → Pkt. -yara.
Skt. āditya → Pkt. āicca 'sun'; locative plural -esu, comparative force as with candesu.
Skt. adhika 'more, surpassing' → Pkt. ahiya; adverbial accusative -aṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Intensifies payāsayarā — 'even more illuminating', pushing the sun-simile past the moon-simile.

wordByWord.etymology: pra + √kāś 'to shine' → Pkt. payāsa 'light, radiance'; + comparative -yara.

wordByWord.notes: First member of the simile-compound sāgara-vara-gambhīra (printed as separate words in this book).
Intensifier within the compound: 'the great/foremost ocean'.
Completes the third simile: 'profound as the great ocean'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. siddha 'accomplished, perfected' < √sidh 'to be fulfilled'; nominative plural.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of the verse; the three similes describe these Siddhas.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. siddhi 'attainment, perfection' < √sidh; accusative singular, object of disantu.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberate yamaka with siddhā: 'the perfected ones grant perfecting'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. mama 'my, to me'; genitive/dative of the first person.
wordByWord.notes: Personalizes the petition — the reciter asks for himself.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. √diś 'to point out, allot, grant' → diśantu; Pkt. disantu, 3rd person plural imperative/optative.

literalTranslation: More spotless than the moons, exceedingly more radiant than the suns, deep as the great ocean — may the Siddhas grant perfection (siddhi) to me.
idiomaticTranslation: Purer than all the moons, more dazzling than all the suns, unfathomable as the mighty ocean — O perfected Siddhas, bestow upon me the final perfection.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This closing gāthā seals the hymn by turning from petition to pure adoration through three comparisons — upamā sharpened into vyatireka, where the subject surpasses its very standard. Two are framed by the Prakrit 'locative of comparison': candesu nimmalayarā and āiccesu payāsayarā literally place the Siddhas 'among the moons/suns' only to declare them more spotless and more radiant than either. The adverb ahiyaṁ ('exceedingly') pushes the second simile past the first — brighter, and brighter still. The third, sāgara-vara-gambhīrā, exchanges light and height for depth: fathomless as the foremost ocean. Note the precise shift of addressee. vv.5–6 petitioned the twenty-four embodied Jinas/Tīrthaṅkaras for grace, health, bodhi and samādhi; v.7 narrows to the siddhā — souls already wholly liberated and bodiless — and asks for the single final thing, siddhi itself. The verse thus completes a ladder: from ārogga–bohilābha–samāhivara (v.6) up to liberation (v.7). The yamaka of siddhā…siddhiṁ ('the perfected grant perfecting') is deliberate: goal and giver share one name. The similes are doctrinally exact, not ornamental. A Siddha is rūpa-less, so 'radiance' and 'spotlessness' cannot be physical: the moon-surpassing purity is freedom from the mala of karma, and the sun-surpassing light is kevala-jñāna, a knowing that needs no external lamp and casts no shadow. The ocean's gambhīra depth images the soul's now-infinite, imperturbable guṇas — vast, still, unfathomable. Where the moon waxes and wanes and the sun sets, the Siddha's purity and light are changeless: the verse invokes the brightest things in nature only to transcend them.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse encodes the siddha-svarūpa: a liberated soul has shed all eight karmas, so its purity exceeds the moon (no mala remains) and its omniscient kevala-jñāna outshines the sun (it illumines every dravya and paryāya without any sense-organ). 'Deep as the great ocean' affirms the infinitude and stability (sthairya) of the chief qualities of the Siddha (ananta-jñāna, ananta-darśana, ananta-vīrya, etc.). Crucially the petition is addressed to bodiless Siddhas who, in strict doctrine, neither act nor confer favours; the optative disantu is therefore best read not as a transactional boon but as the reciter orienting his own resolve toward siddhi — the Siddhas 'grant' precisely by being the realized proof and pattern of the goal.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: In recitation this verse is the summit of Logassa: having named and praised all twenty-four, the reciter now lifts the heart to the final aim and makes it personal (mama, 'for me'). It is also a recitation landmark — the silent one-Logassa measure used for kāyotsarga is held only up to the opening of this verse (candesu nimmalayarā), so a practitioner learns exactly where the counted meditation ends and this closing prayer begins. Chanted slowly, its three images — moon, sun, ocean — give the mind three steadying objects before the single closing request.

crossReferences.text: The bodiless, omniscient nature and infinite qualities of the Siddha
'candesu nimmalayara' as the terminus of the one-Logassa kāyotsarga
crossReferences.source: Āvaśyaka Niryukti g.1532, as cited in Pañca Pratikramaṇa Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit

sources.title: Nirmala / Nirmalā — definitions (incl. Jainism, Prakrit, Sanskrit)
sources.consultedFor: Etymology and semantic range of nirmala (Pkt. nimmala) 'stainless, spotless' underlying nimmalayarā.

sources.title: Siddhi — definitions (incl. Jainism)
sources.consultedFor: Sense of siddhi 'perfection/accomplishment/liberation' and its relation to siddha.

sources.title: Tirthankara (and Siddha / kevala-jnana / moksha)
sources.consultedFor: Doctrinal grounding for the Siddha's omniscience (kevala-jñāna), karmic purity and liberation invoked by the verse's similes.

notes: The locatives candesu/āiccesu are the Prakrit comparative-locative ('than the moons/suns'), not partitive ('among'); both readings circulate, but the comparative is standard here and is reinforced by the -yara (Skt. -tara) comparatives nimmalayarā/payāsayarā. The source book prints 'sāgara vara gambhīrā' as three orthographic words; many recensions write the single compound sāgaravaragambhīrā — the sense (deep as the foremost ocean) is identical. ahiyaṁ (Skt. adhika) is taken adverbially with payāsayarā ('exceedingly more radiant'). disantu (√diś) is the petitionary 3rd-person-plural imperative. All mātrās and the anusvāra of ahiyaṁ/siddhiṁ are preserved verbatim as supplied.
